sentences.text: Yes, of course, we know.
We knew, because we listened to the radio, yeah.
We was so afraid.
We was-- we-- we knows it-- it must be something.
We will be never surviving.
Yes.
You can't.
Yes.
My brother was offered a job here in Australia, in Melbourne, and my mother said, don't leave us.
We are a little family, you know?
We was three together, like three little bird, you know?
And she don't let him go.
And maybe if she let him go, maybe it's-- it's a different life, a different things every day.
Maybe he's not killed, and my mother-- but my mother don't want to separate the family.
We have no money that time, because we using it up everything.
We-- we don't earn any money, you know?
Because they-- they take it away, everything.
We-- we don't survive, because when we go to the ghetto and we live what-- they take us our gold and everything.
They-- you have to put it in, everything what you have.
If you have any gold or-- or dollar, or things, you have to give it to the--
Yes, yes.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
No.
You can't.
You have to.
Have-- we pack up our things.
And how I say?
The-- we only take a bed to the ghetto.
All-- all the other furnishing we put it in my father's place, because he has a big place.
And the-- all the-- the linen things that are all-- everything-- the clothes, the-- the coats and things, we-- we put it in my father storage.
They said if you want, we can put some things away from you.
That's how-- that's how my [NON-ENGLISH]-- my--
--tapestry-- it's still here.

It survived, because two, three, we give it to one of my friend, the other two, three another friend, and my brother's suit, and with one friend, and my mother fur jacket in the other friend, you know?
So we-- we give it to the nice people who asking.
They said, we put something away from you-- to-- to you.
Maybe-- maybe you come back, and then you need it.
And that's it.
So that's what I did.
Oh, yes.
The area was around the synagogue, you know?
And the very poor--
Area, yes.
But we was happy, because we was still alive.
And we have our uncle with us, and my cousins with us, and--
No.
It was no fence.
But it was all lock up gate.
And after 6:00, you can't go out.
A Jewish police boys-- I-- police-- old men who was still there, because all the young people was in the arbeit camp.
Had I go?
I-- I-- I went.
I only stay for few weeks.
And then we have to go move in in that factory.
Yes.
It was one kitchen.
Oh yes, with my-- my-- my uncle-- he-- my mother brother, and his wife, and his two daughters.
Yeah, in-- in-- yes.
It was one bedroom, and was all the beds, [LAUGHS] and one kitchen, and--
Yes.
Yeah.
How many?
Two, four, six-- six of us.
Look.
For how I say to you, we take something with us, what we get-- a little flour, a little fat things, you know?
But how I said to you, my girlfriend mother bring a piece of bread, another girlfriend mother bring a piece of bread, and--
No, no, no, no, no, no.
In the dark-- and they-- they-- the-- with the-- just drop it in in the window, because you can't.
Yes, because the Jewish police have to report it to the authority.
I don't know.
I-- I don't know.
I have no experience of that, you know?
Because I was working.
I have two job.
Even then I still have two job, because I was working in the--
Yes.
And in the morning or when I was have free, I have a friend who has a salon in a-- Hungary, and he-- she gave me a job.
So I--
--go a few hours for sewing, yes.
So I earn a little bit of money, so we can buy some food.
We--
In Ujpest.
No, no.
I have to walk about 1/2 an hour-- good 1/2 an hour to--
Yeah, you have to.
Yes.
Of course, but I was-- I always have a-- a working jacket, and I wear the star on the working jacket.
And when I wanted to go somewhere, I take it off, the jacket, and I go without the star.
Because nobody think I am a Jewish girl, because I was fair.
And so I-- I don't look a Jew.
At maybe to see my girlfriend, you know, to talk to her, because she can't come to the ghetto, you know?
So I was going into her place, that nobody should see me.
But I can't go with the star, because the star straight away show who I am.
And even sometimes we go to the movie.
I take the star off, and we go to the movie.
Because they want I should go with them.
You know, it was-- for them, it was fun.
For me, I go because they wanted I should go.
But I was worried always.
[SIGHS]
Yes.
At that time he-- he give us food.
He give us some food.
And that-- I-- I go to him.
But even where I go to him daytime-- if I go to him, I can wear the star.
But after 6 o'clock or something-- I don't remember-- 5 or 6 o'clock-- I had to take off the star, or I can't go.
No, because he was not a Jew anymore, and his wife was a-- a born Catholic.
So he was a mixed marriage.
He don't need to go to the ghetto.
And that's why he was thinking if nothing will happen to him.
But unfortunately, it is happen to him.
And when that Hungarian [NON-ENGLISH] come to--
--power, yes, they take Miklos Horthy off, and that-- then they take him away and kill him.
Yes.
Yes, of course.
The-- [LAUGHS] the Hungarian films there, because no American film coming anymore, not overseas films; and, of course, the all operettes, and things, you know?
We-- we loved to see it.
And we went with my girlfriends.
Oh, yes, because my mother cousins had three movie theater in Ujpest.
It was a wealthy family, and I don't need to pay.
[LAUGHS] So we go-- I take my girlfriends, and we go to the movies.
And where a new film come, we was there-- the first to see it.
[LAUGHS]
The-- the Al Jolson, the and Sonja Henie with the--
--ice skating.
I love it.
We love it; and that was big things; and Spencer Tracy.
And oh, my sweetheart was Nelson Eddy.
We even put our pocket money together and send him a letter to send us a photo.
So we 10 girls, we get one photo from Eddy Nelson.
And we was so happy.
[LAUGHS] We have a photo, and we keep it in school.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, because she was my father girlfriend in-- in Budapest.
It's cost him a lot of money.
Can you imagine?
For a little Jewish Ujpest-- Ujpest Jewish boy have a girlfriend, Josephine Baker-- she don't do it for nothing.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
That time yes, because that time was not anymore belongs to my uncles, you know?
In the--
The same thing.
Who owned it then?
Non-Jewish people, you know?
They-- they just take it away, and give it to somebody else.
Yes.
[SIGHS] Never come home.
My mother have 35 cousins, and not one survived-- 35.
Because my great-grandmother have two big flat house, and every month, my mother went to get the money.
So all the cousins come together in one of my auntie's place.
And it was 35 cousins-- and not one coming back.
I make-- I-- working in the woven machine, and once I have accident.
And that's how I meet that boy.
Because that boy-- we was like a prisoners, you know-- and to take me to bandage my hand, and getting better, he--
At that man-- that boy was a-- a fire-- fireman.
But they trust him.
And he took me to the doctor and wait for me.
I shouldn't--
Escaping.
And I was all-- of course, I was crying, because I was shame of myself, like a-- like a criminal that-- I was 18, 19-- 19, yes.
In the factory.
I-- I hurt my finger, and coming off a top--
No, not the nail, the-- the-- the meat, [LAUGHS] and I still have it, the--
A-- a-- a doctor.
And every day, I have to go to the fresh--
Band-aid-- the dressing.
And that boy took me--
Always the same boy.
Yeah.
And he can keep his eyes on me to-- I shouldn't escape.
And he feel so sorry for me, because I was always crying.
And he said, oh, don't cry.
Don't cry-- nevermind-- nevermind.
And that's how I can escape that time in the factory.
We have a camp-- where is the girls.
We live in the double-decker beds.
And we was all the young girls together.
And the separate place-- not with the-- and when we working, when the bomb coming to the English airplanes and the Americans, they have a bunker where we go.
But the Jewish girls have to go separate than the other workers, because we worked together with the other-- the other workers.
Non-Jew, yes.
Supervising.
If-- if somebody have no qualification, then she working with us.
What you mean?
Yeah.
Yes, of course.
Yes, but they was free.
They can go, but not with the stars.
We was together, but we have the stars so that everybody can see it.
Because it was that big-- like my hand-- have to-- was a measurement-- how big have to be the star.
Yes, I think I get some money.
I think I get some money.
No, no, no, no.
Because they give us the food.
They--
[LAUGHS]
How can you imagine?
And they give us the shelter, you know, to where to-- And you could still go freely in and out of the ghetto?
No, no, no.
No, no-- not in that stage.
Of course.
Although they bring it from the countryside, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the--
The-- the very strong police there.
Of course, yes, of course-- and
--bayonet, you know the things.
And-- and they-- we-- we couldn't go out.
And my mother cooking something what was my favorite, and bring it to me in the afternoon-- but she can't come in.
She just give me in the fence a little bit of soup and things to-- to have a little bit of home cooking to her little girl.
And then one day, she come and said to me, I heard they take us away.
I said oh, no.
It's impossible.
Now what-- what they want with the old women and the old mens, you know?
Because no young people was anymore in the ghetto.
And-- but if I say to her that time to go somewhere, maybe she's still alive, and I never forgive myself.
And I said to her, no, Mommy.
You just go home.
Nothing will happen.
And I never see her anymore.
Back to the-- back to the ghetto.
They take her.
Next morning, 6 o'clock, they take her away.
How do I know?
My-- my father give some money of that man who was in-- working in the same place where I was working.
And he bring me the news.
And he said--
Non-Jewish, non-Jewish.
Yeah.
To the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, to--
Brick factory to-- to ready to Auschwitz.
And that's why I was escaping, because I want to go after her.
[SIGHS] We was ready to the wagon in the factory.
They come to us in the morning.
They said, put your things together, because you're going somewhere.
So then they cut our hair shorter to have it-- you can see everybody can see it.
They are a Jewish girls, you know?
Then it was a shorter, I had.
[SIGHS]
And of course, I have the star always in my working blouse.
And I put it on.
And we're going in a line.
And they take us to the wagon.
That was in June, early June.
No, it's started the hot weather-- spring-- summertime coming.
And they take us to the wagon.
Walk-- walk to the-- the wagon, come into the factory.
Because that was a big factory.
They have their own-- own train line, you know?
So it was the-- the train come out, and they put us in.
To the wagon.
[LAUGHS] Like they take the sheeps, and the-- the things, you know, the animals-- exactly the same wagon.
Ugh.
[SIGHS] You can't count it.
We was-- we was like a herrings.
But then a [? siren ?] started.
Siren.
And they said, everybody out!
Everybody out!
German soldiers and Hungarian soldiers.
And they said, everybody out and go to the bunker, the-- the Jewish bunker.
And I said to myself, no, not me.
I run to the non-Jewish bunker, I take off my blouse where I have the star.
And I have a scarf.
And I put it on my head.
And I go to the toilet.
And I don't move-- when everybody settled down.
And then I leave my parcel there, and I walk out.
And I started to run to the gate.
And then that boy who was taking me to the doctor's--
In the fire engine.
He was standing there and look at me.
What-- what-- what-- what did I do over there?
And I shake my head.
I said, oh, don't.
And he turned around.
And between the two soldier with the gun, I run out in the gate, and I take a big breath.
And I run to my father place.
Nobody was in the street, because it-- it was a bomb--
This is it, yeah-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
[SIGHS] And I went to my father place.
And--
That was oh, so quick!
You know, they-- they don't realize that what I doing.
It was the gates open, and the two soldiers standing, and all the other soldiers around it.
No, they can't.
They have to--
To the gate, you know?
They look after the gate.
And even that boy, he don't.
Because he have to watch who is going out and who is coming in.
And they-- they don't realize it what I doing.
It was so quick.
And they just-- a-- a-- a second, and I was out.
They even don't-- don't know what's happening.
And I turned straight away to the other street, you know?
And I was disappearing.
Nobody came after you?
No.
They don't realize it-- I belongs to them-- to they.
When they are realize it, I was so far away.
I go to my father.
How far?
[SIGHS] About 20 minutes running.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Have no-- every-- when was a-- a--
--air raid, that it's everything stopped-- no moving, no-- no horses, nothing.
Everybody stopped.
And everybody run to the bunker.
No.
It was--
No, no, no, no, no, no, nobody was in the street-- no.
Yes, I ran to my father, and when they see me, they get the shock in their life.
And [SIGHS] my father said, what-- what you doing here?
What you doing here?
I said, I run away.
He said, but you can't do it.
I have my family, my-- my son, my wife.
I said, all right, let me sit down and catch my breath, you know?
Because I was running.
And then-- He didn't want you to stay?
No.
He said-- then he go to the other room with his wife, and then he come back and said, you can't stay here.
I can't risk my family.
I said, I am not your family?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He don't answer me.
And then the bell ring, because he has a lock-up gate, you know, lock-up piece.
And it was that man come to my father whom he give money to tell him what's happening to me.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH].
Now they take the girls away.
Your girl is in the wagon.
She going to out.
So he give him again money and said to him, thank you very much.
And again, if you heard something, you come.
So then he gone.
And I said to my father, what you want I should do?
He said, you can't stay here.
I said, all right.
Give me a piece of bread.
[LAUGHS] And I know my mother have a friend in-- a lady friend in Rakospalota.
That's where your holiday house was?
No.
No.
That's a-- that's a next town--
--like a Bondi and Paddington, so--
Suburb, yeah.
I said to him, I-- I go to her.
Maybe she give me--
Non-Jewish friend, yeah.
So I tell him the address, and there I go.
And I went to there.
I walk about 1-- 1 hour, 1 and a 1/2 hour.
Nobody stopped me, because I was-- have-- you know, that modern way I put that scarf on, you know, to-- like a holiday girl.
And I have a sleeveless frock on, you know?
Because I haven't got my working things on.
And I was a pretty girl.
And nobody think I am Jewish.
And I-- I walk.
And I walk, and I [SIGHS] went to her.
And she said to me--
Shh-- well, I wanted to-- Speltzer or something like that.
I wanted to tell you quickly, and I forgot it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Something asha-- something started.
She was so surprised.
And she said, look.
I-- I would like to have you, but the air soldiers in the corners that-- who-- who shot the airplanes, you know, they're here.
And they're coming here to have water and things.
You can't stay here.
I said, all right.
But let me sit down.
[LAUGHS] And I eat my bread, what my father give me.
And I was crying, of course.
And I don't know what to do.
And then the door was open, and my father wife come after me.
She said to me, your father send me.
You have to go back to the factory, because they stop the transport.
It's not going.
So you should go back.
And I say, it's no other way.
Where shall I go?
What shall I do?
So I go back.
I-- I started to walk with her to go back to the factory.
Because-- Did she walk also?
Yes, she walk also.
And so [SIGHS] we don't talk anything.
And then when we come in the corner, a pointed corner--
--section, and that way you go to factory, and that way you go to my father place.
And I said to her, you know, I want to see my father again.
She said, no.
You have to come with me.
Your father said to me I take you back.
I said, I don't go back.
I go and see my father.
He is my father.
So she was very upset, but she see she can't talk to me.
So we went home.
OK, thank you.
